---
title: "Cameron & Trivedi — Ch. 1: Stata basics (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Colin Cameron
- Pravin Trivedi
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

This chapter is a translation of *Microeconometrics Using Stata* Chapter 1,
routed through the [`StatEcon`](https://github.com/ElvisCasco/StatEcon) package
so every Stata command shown in the book (`summarize`, `regress`, `ereturn list`, …)
maps to a single Julia call with Stata-style printed output.

[Microeconometrics with R](https://ycroissant.github.io/micsr_book/)
· [UCLA Stata modules](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/modules/)
· [Github](https://github.com/thousandoaks?tab=repositories)

# Julia setup

Only the packages actually used in this chapter are loaded. The `stata_*`
commands come from `StatEcon`; everything else is standard Julia stack.

```{julia}
using StatEcon                # stata_summarize, stata_regress, ereturn_list, ...
const DATA = let c = filter(d -> isdir(d) && (isfile(joinpath(d, "auto.dta")) || isdir(joinpath(d, "musr"))), ["../../data", "../data", "data"]); isempty(c) ? error("StatEcon example data not found; run this notebook from its own folder (examples/Cameron_Trivedi) or the repo root.") : first(c) end
using DataFrames
using ReadStatTables
using FixedEffectModels
using StatsModels
using Statistics
using StatsBase               # for a few direct StatsBase calls in §1.6.2
using CategoricalArrays
using Random

# Tiny helper: unwrap a ReadStatTables.LabeledValue -> its underlying number.
# `stata_summarize` handles this internally, but a couple of hand-written
# cells below still touch labelled columns directly (factor variables,
# rep78/foreign interactions).
_rawval(x) = hasproperty(x, :value) ? x.value : x
```

# 1 Stata basics

## 1.1 Interactive use

## 1.2 Documentation

### 1.2.1 Stata manuals

### 1.2.2 Additional Stata resources

### 1.2.3 The help command

### 1.2.4 The search, findit, and hsearch commands

## 1.3 Command syntax and operators

### 1.3.1 Basic command syntax

### 1.3.2 Example: The summarize command

```{julia}
#=
sysuse auto.dta
summarize
=#
auto = DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/auto.dta"))
typeof(auto)
```

```{julia}
#=
summarize mpg price weight , separator(2)
=#
# summarize   (all numeric vars, like `summarize` with no varlist)
stata_summarize(auto)
```

```{julia}
# summarize mpg price weight
stata_summarize(auto, [:mpg, :price, :weight])
```

```{julia}
# summarize price, detail
stata_summarize(auto, :price; detail = true)
```

### 1.3.3 Example: The regress command

```{julia}
#=
regress mpg price weight
=#
model1 = stata_regress(auto, y = :mpg, x = [:price, :weight])
```

```{julia}
#=
by foreign: regress mpg price weight if weight < 4000, vce(robust)
=#
for g in groupby(filter(r -> r.weight < 4000, auto), :foreign)
    println("\nforeign = $(first(g.foreign))")
    stata_regress(DataFrame(g), y = :mpg, x = [:price, :weight]; vce = :robust)
end
```

```{julia}
#=
quietly regress mpg price weight
=#
# `quietly` -> just discard the printed table.
model_quiet = redirect_stdout(devnull) do
    stata_regress(auto, y = :mpg, x = [:price, :weight])
end;
```

```{julia}
#=
* Factor variable for rep78 - base category is omitted
clear
sysuse auto
summarize i.rep78
=#
auto.rep78_cat = CategoricalArrays.categorical(auto.rep78)
println("Summary of rep78 indicator levels:")
println(combine(groupby(auto, :rep78_cat), nrow => :count))
```

```{julia}
#=
* Factor variable for rep78 - no category is omitted
summarize ibn.rep78
=#
# summarize i.rep78  -- expand rep78 into 0/1 dummies (drop missings), summarize each
vals = [_rawval(v) for v in skipmissing(auto.rep78)]
levs = sort(unique(vals))
dummies = DataFrame((Symbol("$(Int(l)).rep78") => Int.(vals .== l) for l in levs)...)
stata_summarize(dummies)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Factor variables for interaction between two categorical variables
summarize i.rep78#i.foreign, allbaselevels
=#
println("Counts for each level of rep78:")
for lev in sort(unique(auto.rep78))
    n = sum(auto.rep78 .== lev)
    println("  rep78 == $lev : N = $n, Mean = $(round(mean(auto.rep78 .== lev), digits=4))")
end
```

```{julia}
#=
* Factor variables for interaction between categorical and continuous variables
summarize i.rep78#c.weight
=#
# summarize i.rep78#i.foreign, allbaselevels  -> one dummy per (rep78, foreign) cell
sub       = dropmissing(auto[:, [:rep78, :foreign]])
rep78v    = [_rawval(v) for v in sub.rep78]
foreignv  = [_rawval(v) for v in sub.foreign]
rlevs, flevs = sort(unique(rep78v)), sort(unique(foreignv))
dummies = DataFrame((Symbol("$(Int(r)).rep78#$(Int(f)).foreign") =>
                     Int.((rep78v .== r) .& (foreignv .== f))
                     for r in rlevs, f in flevs)...)
stata_summarize(dummies)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Factor variables for interaction between two continuous variables
regress mpg price c.weight c.weight#c.weight, noheader
=#
# summarize i.rep78#c.weight
# For each level r of rep78: weight when rep78==r, else 0 (drop rows where rep78 is missing)
sub    = dropmissing(auto[:, [:rep78, :weight]])
rep78v = [_rawval(v) for v in sub.rep78]
wv     = Float64.(sub.weight)
rlevs  = sort(unique(rep78v))
inter  = DataFrame((Symbol("$(Int(r)).rep78#c.weight") =>
                    ifelse.(rep78v .== r, wv, 0.0) for r in rlevs)...)
stata_summarize(inter)
```

```{julia}
# regress mpg price c.weight c.weight#c.weight
# NOTE: auto.dta stores weight as Int16 — squaring Int16 overflows silently,
# so cast to Float64 first to match Stata's numeric precision.
auto.weight_sq = Float64.(auto.weight) .^ 2
stata_regress(auto, y = :mpg, x = [:price, :weight, :weight_sq])
```

### 1.3.4 Abbreviations, case sensitivity, and wildcards

```{julia}
#=
summarize t*
=#
# summarize t*     (variables starting with t)
stata_summarize(auto, "t*")
```

```{julia}
# summarize *e     (variables ending with e)
stata_summarize(auto, "*e")
```

```{julia}
# summarize price-weight    (range of columns, by column order)
stata_summarize(auto, "price-weight")
```

### 1.3.5 Arithmetic, relational, and logical operators

```{julia}
#=
display -2*(9/ (8+2-7))^2
=#
println(-2 * (9 / (8 + 2 - 7))^2)
```

```{julia}
#=
display 2/0
=#
println(2 / 0)      # Julia returns Inf
```

```{julia}
#=
regress mpg price weight if weight <= 4000 & price <= 10000
=#
sub = filter(r -> r.weight <= 4000 && r.price <= 10000, auto)
stata_regress(sub, y = :mpg, x = [:price, :weight])
```

### 1.3.6 Error messages

```{julia}
#=
*regress mpg notthere
=#
# regress mpg notthere  -- would produce an error, so left commented out.
# try
#     stata_regress(auto, y = :mpg, x = [:notthere])
# catch e
#     println("Error: ", e)
# end
```

## 1.4 Do-files and log files

### 1.4.1 Writing a do-file

### 1.4.2 Running do-files

```{julia}
#=
*do example.do
=#
# do example.do  -- In Julia, use include("example.jl")
# include("example.jl")
```

### 1.4.3 Log files

```{julia}
#=
*log using example.txt, text replace
=#
# log using example.txt -- In Julia, redirect output to a file:
# open("example.txt", "w") do io
#     redirect_stdout(io) do
#         # commands here
#     end
# end
```

### 1.4.4 A three-step process

1. Create or change the do-file.
2. Execute the do-file in Stata.
3. Read the resulting log with a text editor.

### 1.4.5 Comments and long lines

```{julia}
#=
* Demonstrate use of comments
* This program reads in system file auto.dta and gets summary statistics
clear                                     // Remove data from memory
sysuse ///
auto.dta
summarize
=#
# In Julia, comments use #
auto = DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/auto.dta"))
DataFrames.describe(auto)
```

```{julia}
auto = DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/auto.dta"))
stata_summarize(auto)
```

### 1.4.6 Different implementations of Stata

```{julia}
#=
*set matsize 300
=#
# set matsize 300  -- not needed in Julia (no matrix size limit)
```

## 1.5 Scalars and matrices

### 1.5.1 Scalars

```{julia}
#=
scalar a = 2*3
scalar b = "2 times 3 = "
display b a
=#
a = 2 * 3
b = "2 times 3 = "
println(b, a)
```

### 1.5.2 Matrices

```{julia}
#=
matrix define A = ( 1,2,3 \ 4,5,6 )
matrix list A
=#
A = [1 2 3; 4 5 6]
display(A)
```

```{julia}
#=
scalar c = A[2,3]
display c
=#
c = A[2, 3]
println(c)
```

## 1.6 Using results from Stata commands

### 1.6.1 Using results from the r-class command summarize

```{julia}
#=
summarize mpg
=#
stata_summarize(auto, :mpg)
```

```{julia}
#=
return list
=#
# Stata's `return list` after `summarize mpg`: the r() scalars.
r_mean = mean(auto.mpg)
r_sd   = std(auto.mpg)
r_min  = minimum(auto.mpg)
r_max  = maximum(auto.mpg)
r_N    = length(auto.mpg)
r_sum  = sum(auto.mpg)
println("r(N)    = ", r_N)
println("r(mean) = ", r_mean)
println("r(Var)  = ", r_sd^2)
println("r(sd)   = ", r_sd)
println("r(min)  = ", r_min)
println("r(max)  = ", r_max)
println("r(sum)  = ", r_sum)
```

```{julia}
#=
quietly summarize mpg
scalar range = r(max) - r(min)
display "Sample range = " range
=#
range_mpg = r_max - r_min
println("Sample range = ", range_mpg)
```

```{julia}
# Save a result as a scalar
mpgmean = r_mean
```

### 1.6.2 Using results from the e-class command regress

```{julia}
#=
regress mpg price weight
=#
model_ols = stata_regress(auto, y = :mpg, x = [:price, :weight])
```

```{julia}
#=
ereturn list
=#
ereturn_list(model_ols);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Use of e() where scalar
scalar r2 = e(mss) / ( e(mss) + e(rss) )
display "r-squared = " r2
=#
println("Number of obs = ", StatsBase.nobs(model_ols))
println("R-squared     = ", round(StatsBase.r2(model_ols), digits = 6))
println("Adj R-squared = ", round(StatsBase.adjr2(model_ols), digits = 6))
println("Coefficients  = ", StatsBase.coef(model_ols))
println("Std errors    = ", StatsBase.stderror(model_ols))
```

```{julia}
# r-squared computed manually from e(rss) and total SS
tss       = sum((auto.mpg .- mean(auto.mpg)).^2)
rss       = StatsBase.deviance(model_ols)
r2_manual = 1 - rss / tss
println("r-squared = ", round(r2_manual, digits = 6))
```

```{julia}
#=
* Use of e() where matrix
matrix best  = e(b)
scalar bprice = best[1,1]
matrix Vest  = e(V)
scalar Vprice = Vest[1,1]
scalar tprice = bprice/sqrt(Vprice)
display "t statistic for H0: b_price = 0 is " tprice
=#
best   = StatsBase.coef(model_ols)
bprice = best[2]                       # coefficient on price
Vest   = StatsBase.vcov(model_ols)
Vprice = Vest[2, 2]
tprice = bprice / sqrt(Vprice)
println("t statistic for H0: b_price = 0 is ", round(tprice, digits = 4))
```

## 1.7 Global and local macros

### 1.7.1 Global macros

```{julia}
#=
global xlist price weight
regress mpg $xlist , noheader
=#
xlist   = [:price, :weight]
model_g = stata_regress(auto, y = :mpg, x = xlist)
```

### 1.7.2 Local macros

```{julia}
#=
local xlist "price weight"
regress mpg `xlist', noheader
=#
xlist   = [:price, :weight]
model_l = stata_regress(auto, y = :mpg, x = xlist)
```

```{julia}
#=
local y mpg
regress `y' `xlist', noheader
=#
y       = :mpg
model_l2 = stata_regress(auto, y = y, x = xlist)
```

```{julia}
#=
local z = 2+2
display `z'
=#
z = 2 + 2
println(z)
```

### 1.7.3 Scalar or macro?

```{julia}
#=
global b 3
local  c 4
scalar d = 5
clear
display $b _skip(3) `c'
=#
# In Julia, variables persist until they go out of scope.
b = 3
c = 4
d = 5
println(b, "   ", c)
```

```{julia}
#=
display d
=#
println(d)
```

```{julia}
#=
clear all
display $b _skip(3) `c'
=#
# After `clear all` in Stata, only globals/locals survive; in Julia everything
# in the current scope survives, so this just prints the same values.
println(b, "   ", c)
```

```{julia}
#=
* display the scalar
* display d
=#
# println(d) still works in Julia (no `clear` semantics).
```

## 1.8 Looping commands

```{julia}
#=
* Make artificial dataset of 100 observations on 4 uniform variables
clear
set obs 100
set seed 10101
generate x1var = runiform()
generate x2var = runiform()
generate x3var = runiform()
generate x4var = runiform()
=#
Random.seed!(10101)
df_loop = DataFrame(
    x1var = rand(100),
    x2var = rand(100),
    x3var = rand(100),
    x4var = rand(100),
)
```

```{julia}
#=
generate sum = x1var + x2var + x3var + x4var
summarize sum
=#
df_loop.sum_var = df_loop.x1var .+ df_loop.x2var .+ df_loop.x3var .+ df_loop.x4var
stata_summarize(df_loop, :sum_var)
```

### 1.8.1 The foreach loop

```{julia}
#=
quietly replace sum = 0
foreach var of varlist x1var x2var x3var x4var {
    quietly replace sum = sum + `var'
}
summarize sum
=#
df_loop.sum_var .= 0.0
for var in [:x1var, :x2var, :x3var, :x4var]
    df_loop.sum_var .+= df_loop[!, var]
end
stata_summarize(df_loop, :sum_var)
```

### 1.8.2 The forvalues loop

```{julia}
#=
quietly replace sum = 0
forvalues i = 1/4 {
    quietly replace sum = sum + x`i'var
}
summarize sum
=#
df_loop.sum_var .= 0.0
for i in 1:4
    df_loop.sum_var .+= df_loop[!, Symbol("x$(i)var")]
end
stata_summarize(df_loop, :sum_var)
```

```{julia}
#=
// Not in the book
quietly replace sum = 0
foreach i of numlist 1 2 3 4 {
    quietly replace sum = sum + x`i'var
}
summarize sum
=#
df_loop.sum_var .= 0.0
for i in [1, 2, 3, 4]
    df_loop.sum_var .+= df_loop[!, Symbol("x$(i)var")]
end
stata_summarize(df_loop, :sum_var)
```

### 1.8.3 The while loop

```{julia}
#=
quietly replace sum = 0
local i 1
while `i' <= 4 {
    quietly replace sum = sum + x`i'var
    local i = `i' + 1
}
summarize sum
=#
df_loop.sum_var .= 0.0
i = 1
while i <= 4
    df_loop.sum_var .+= df_loop[!, Symbol("x$(i)var")]
    global i += 1
end
stata_summarize(df_loop, :sum_var)
```
